front: What is the definition of a dense subset in a metric space?
back: A subset D of a metric space (M, d) is dense if its closure is equal to the entire space, i.e., cl(D) = M. Equivalently, every point in M is either in D or is a limit point of D.

front: What is the definition of a separable metric space?
back: A metric space is called separable if it contains a countable, dense subset. That is, there exists a sequence (or finite set) of points in the space such that every open set contains at least one point from this sequence.

front: State a key property of dense sets related to continuous functions.
back: If two continuous functions f and g from a metric space X to a metric space Y agree on a dense subset D of X (i.e., f(d)=g(d) for all d in D), and Y is Hausdorff (e.g., any metric space), then f = g on all of X.

front: Provide an example of a separable metric space and a non-separable metric space.
back:
Separable: The real numbers R with the usual metric. The set of rational numbers Q is a countable dense subset.
Non-Separable: The space l∞ of all bounded sequences of real numbers with the supremum metric. It is not separable because its standard basis vectors are an uncountable set where the distance between any two distinct vectors is 1.

front: How is the concept of denseness related to the closure of a set?
back: The closure of a set A is the smallest closed set containing A. A set D is dense if and only if its closure is the entire space. This means that for any point x in the space and any ε > 0, there exists a point d in D such that d(x, d) < ε.
